characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: A banker sentenced to life in prison in 1947 for the murder of his wife and her lover. Adaptational Angst Upgrade: Andy rarely discusses his wife in the novella. The film adds a conversation with Red (taking place 20 years after her death) where he admits he feelshis inability to express his emotionsdrove her into another man’s arms,leading to her murder. The movie also explains that the hayfield hehid his letter to Red inwas where he proposed, showingAndy still reminisces about her. Adaptational Villainy: A minor example, as he doesn’t go through with it in either case, but the film changes why he had the gun that implicated him in the murders. In the novella, the only person he considered harming with it washimself. In the movie, he was so drunk he can't fully remember what he was thinking but believes he was considering scaring his wife and her lover (though not killing them). This alteration was likely made to accompany the film leaving Andy’s involvement in the shootings ambiguous until Tommy recounts Blatch’s confession (unlike the novella, which was open about his innocence from the start). Affably Evil: Andy is seen by the guards sans Hadley as a polite, helpful, and normally reasonable man who murdered his cheating wife in a lapse of judgement, with the novella even detailing they sometimes forget he's a convict. The Blatch revelation makes it clear only the "affable" bit applies. Ambiguous Situation:For most of the film, we're left confused as to whether or not Andy did kill his wife and her lover, the scene of him on the night of murders ending before the part where he turns around and gets back in his car.It's unclear if Andy's letter to the Portland Daily Bugle revealed why Norton had Tommy killed by mentioning Elmo Blatch confessed to Tommy that he was the one who killed Mrs. Dufresne and Glenn Quentin. That being said, with Tommy dead and unable to tell the story himself, it would be hearsay coming from anyone else and there's no physical evidence to

characters.name: Andy
characters.description: A banker sentenced to life in prison in 1947 for the murder of his wife and her lover. Adaptational Angst Upgrade: Andy rarely discusses his wife in the novella. The film adds a conversation with Red (taking place 20 years after her death) where he admits he feelshis inability to express his emotionsdrove her into another man’s arms,leading to her murder. The movie also explains that the hayfield hehid his letter to Red inwas where he proposed, showingAndy still reminisces about her. The Blatch revelation makes it clear only the "affable" bit applies. That being said, with Tommy dead and unable to tell the story himself, it would be hearsay coming from anyone else and there's no physical evidence to

characters.name: Red
characters.description: A contraband smuggler, imprisoned since 1927 for murder, who befriends Andy. Adaptational Nice Guy: In the novella, Red deliberately kills his wife by cutting the brake lines to her car (He also accidentally killed a neighbor and her baby who hitched a ride). In the movie, the only thing he says about the murder he committed was that he was a "stupid kid," and it's implied to be part of a robbery that went wrong, which would be far easier for the audience to forgive. He’s also more clearly remorseful for his crimes than his character in the novella. Anti-Hero: Red is a convicted murderer and a rather mercenary smuggler, but he's not really a bad guy. He's a good friend to Andy, the only inmate to show genuine remorse for his crime, and disgusted by violent inmates like the Sisters. The Atoner: The only one in Shawshank willing to own up to his guilt, he spends 40 years in prison making up for his crime. Brutal Honesty: Towards the end, once he's lost his best friend, Red has become so tired and bitter of the endless cycle of his parole hearings and so inured to prison life that he finally tells the parole board exactly what he thinks of both them and himself. It'simpliedthis candor is what finally gets him paroled.Board Member:Ellis Boyd Redding, your files say you've served 40 years of a life sentence. Do you feel you've been rehabilitated?Red:Rehabilitated? Well, now let me see. You know, I don't have any idea what that means.Board Member:Well, it means that you're ready to rejoin society—Red:I know what you think it means, sonny. To me, it's just a made-up word. A politician's word, so young fellas like yourself can wear a suit and a tie, and have a job. What do you really want to know? Am I sorry for what I did?Board Member:Well, are you?Red:There's not a day goes by I don't feel regret. Not because I'm in here, or because you think I should. I look back on the way I was then: a young, stupid kid who committed that terrible crime. I want to talk to him. I want t

characters.name: Brooks
characters.description: The elderly prison librarian, imprisoned since 1905 and librarian since 1912. Ascended Extra: Brooks is mentioned only in passing in the novella, whereas in the film he plays a very significant role in both Andy and Red's character development. Beware the Nice Ones: Brooks is one of the most gentle prisoners at Shawshank (relatively speaking, at least). That doesn't stop him from holding a knife to Heywood's neck and even breaking the skin, all just to avoid leaving Shawshank. Also, he's been in prison for decades for a reason: murder. Big Fish in a Bigger Ocean: A deeply tragic example. He is anOld Convictwho has been in prison for fifty years. As the prison librarian and for a long time the only one who has gone to college, as well as for being aNice Guyin general, he is very respected by the rest of the inmates. When he is paroled, he is given a menial job at a convenience store and gets no respect from anyone. It leads to him killing himself, unable to adjust to the modern world. Even before his parole, Red understands his situation:Red: In here he's an important man, an educated man. Out there he's nothin'. A used-up con with arthritis in both hands. Probably couldn't get a library card if he tried. Big Ol' Eyebrows: Massive ginger and gray eyebrows that play a huge role in his expressive face.

characters.name: Heywood
characters.description: A member of Red's gang of long-serving convicts. Book Dumb: His lack of knowledge in literature makes for a hilarious scene in which he misnames several books and authors. Also seeInnocently Insensitivebelow. Break Them by Talking: He taunts Fat Ass with tales ofPrison Rapeto make him break down and cry, so he can win the "new fish" bet. Butt-Monkey: Heywood provides the brunt of the film's comedy relief mostly by being the butt of the most jokes (such as the horse apple sequence). Canon Foreigner: Heywood and the rest of Red's gang aren't in the book.

characters.name: Tommy
characters.description: A young convict imprisoned for burglary in 1965. Adaptational Attractiveness: His novella counterpart self-deprecatingly says his face is not much, and he loses three of his teeth when a guard knocks him out. He's given a more boyish appearance in the film and the incident with the guard is removed. Adaptational Heroism: In the novella, Tommy keeps quiet about Andy's innocence in exchange for a transfer to a more comfortable prison that allows weekend visits for his family, leading to Andy spendinganother 12 years in Shawshank before escaping. In the film he resolves to help Andyonly for Norton and Hadley to murder Tommy before he can testify. Not to mention even when the Warden implies he'll make life hard for Tommy if he does testify, Tommy is adamant about helping Andy.Tommy:Just give me that chance. Book Dumb: He needs lots of help with passing his GED, but is also willing to learn and proves surprisingly capable once he does. Death by Adaptation: In the novella, Tommy isn't killed by Norton and Hadley, but rather given a transfer to a minimum-security prison in exchange for keeping quiet about Andy's innocence.

characters.name: Bogs
characters.description: The head of "the Sisters" gang and a prison rapist. Adaptational Badass: Villainous version. In the novella, Red notes that Bogs and his gang did assault Andy occasionally, but mostly left him alone because he gives them a hard fight every time, and the Sisters prefer easier prey. In the film he's more of aBlood Knight. He targets Andy deliberately, and at one point tells him, "Good. You fight. It's better that way." Adaptational Weapon Swap: He carries a shiv in the film instead of a straight razor, presumably to make his threat to stab Andy more plausible. Ain't Too Proud to Beg: He begs Hadley to stop and cries out for help when during his own attack. It doesn't work. And I Must Scream: He eventually gets beaten up so badly he's left permanently paralyzed and has to be fed through a tube while lying in bed for the rest of his life.

characters.name: Blatch
characters.description: A career criminal who shared a cell with Tommy in another prison. Adaptation Name Change: In the novella, it'sElwoodBlatch, nicknamed "El." Ambiguous Situation: The novella leaves it a bit questionable whether he really did the murders, as he gets a few details wrong and is pegged by multiple people as definitely capable of making something like that up, on top of the massive coincidence on the timing. The movie makes it more clear that he did. Ax-Crazy: Justlistento the way he reminisces killing Andy's wife and her lover for proof that he's this. Bald of Evil: In the novella, Tommy describes him as "mostly bald" (In the movie, his hairline seems to be pretty high up there, but he doesn't seem to be what you would call "bald").

characters.name: Floyd
characters.description: The Big Guy: He's the tallest member of Red's group of friends. Deadpan Snarker: His wit is as dry as the rock of Shawshank's walls.Heywood:(reading off the book title)Count of Monte Crisco.Floyd:That'sCristo, you dumb shit. Hidden Depths: As seen above, he's apparently familiar withThe Count of Monte Cristo, at least enough to know that Heywood's gotten the title wrong. Number Two: He's sort of Red's unofficial second-in-command, or at least the one he respects the most among his fellow guys. OOC Is Serious Business: The only time we see Floyd seriously worried is when he runs down the stairs in a near-panic to get Andy and Red because Brooks has a knife to Heywood's throat.

characters.name: Fat Ass
characters.description: Cruel and Unusual Death: Beaten to death by Hadley his first night in prison. And it was a slow, lingering death in the infirmary with no doctor to help him. I Want My Mommy!: Utters this as he's breaking down in tears. No Name Given: His real name is never revealed, he's only known by the nickname Heywood gave him, which is even listed in the film's credits.Andy: What was his name?Heywood: What did you say?Andy: I was just wondering if anybody knew his name.Heywood: Fuck do you care, new fish? Doesn't fuckin' matter what his name was. He's dead.

characters.name: Norton
characters.description: The prison's unbelievably corrupt warden. 0% Approval Rating:EVERYONEin the prison hates his guts. Hell, even the guards don't seem to miss him when heeats his gun. Adaptational Karma:In the novella, he merely resigns in embarrassment following Andy's escape, with his criminal deeds going undiscovered. The film changes things so that Andy exposes his crimes after escaping, leading to Norton shooting himself to avoid arrest. Adaptational Villainy: A small but very crucial change is made the film to turn him from an evil character to apurelyevil one. Norton isn't any less of a corrupt, hypocritical bastard in the novella and he still suppresses evidence of Andy's innocence out of both greed and spite, but he doesn't go so far as to have Tommy murdered to cover it up, instead buying his silence by transferring him to a cushier prison with a weekend furlough program. Arch-Enemy: Becomes this to Andy Dufresne by systematically ruining his life, just because he wanted to keep Andy in prison to cook the books.

characters.name: Hadley
characters.description: The brutal captain of the prison guards. Adaptational Attractiveness: In the novella, Hadley is described as "shambling" and "horse-faced." In the movie, he's portrayed by handsome, intimidating, straight-backedClancy Brownin his prime. Adaptational Villainy: Like Norton, he's definitely a nasty piece of work in the novella, but is never stated to have taken anyone's life during his time working at Shawshank, and is forced to retire due to a heart attack before Tommy ever arrives at the prison. Ax-Crazy: He's got a lot of issues under that thin lid of calm, considering that he beats and kills the inmates in Shawshank over minor infractions. Bad Guys Do the Dirty Work: Though it was for selfish reasons, Hadley unquestionably did Andy a favor by getting rid ofBogs.

characters.name: Linda Dufresne and Glenn Quentin
characters.description: Andy's estranged wife and her golf pro lover. Andy was sent to Shawshank for their murders. Absence Makes the Heart Go Yonder: Emotional absence. Andy blames himself for his wife's murder, as he's always been a very emotionally reserved man (as noted by the judge, who mentions it during sentencing, condemning him as a cold, remorseless murderer). He believes he drove her into another man's arms. Boom, Headshot!: Were both shot in the head by Blatch, though it was presumably post-mortem. Flat Character: While Andy's dialogue gives insight into Linda's character, we know nothing about Glenn aside from the fact he was a country club golf pro Linda was cheating on Andy with. Killed Offscreen: Their deaths are never shown onscreen, the only time we see them being when they enter Glenn's residence at the country club.

characters.name: 1946 D.A.
characters.description: The district attorney who prosecuted Andy for the murders of Linda Dufresne and Glenn Quentin. Adaptational Heroism: In the novella, he hopes to use Andy's trial to help him in the political world. The film doesn't paint him as anything other than an honest man trying to get two murder victims justice. Cool Old Guy: The film depicts him as a well-spoken and intelligent older prosecutor. Entertainingly Wrong: On paper, he has anOpen-and-Shut Case: Andy admits to buying a .38 caliber revolver before the murders of Mrs. Dufresne and Glenn Quentin, both of the victims had .38 caliber bullets in their bodies, the tire tracks outside the crime scene match Andy's car, the bourbon bottle left outside the crime scene has Andy's fingerprints on them, and Andy had a clear motive, being an angry husband who'd been cheated on and had a very bitter argument with his wife just before her death. The only fact he gets wrong is the identity of the murderer. Everyone Has Standards: He invokes this when describing what he believes to be Andy's murders, saying that while his wife and her lover had done something wrong, they didn't deserve to die for it and drawing a clear distinction between an understandable if still wrong crime of passion and cold-blooded murder as he believes Andy committed.

characters.name: 1946 Judge
characters.description: The judge who sentenced Andy for the murders of Linda Dufresne and Glenn Quentin Entertainingly Wrong: Refers to Andy as an "icy and remorseless man," when in fact Andy is innocent and merely has a reserved personality. No Name Given: His name is never mentioned. Small Role, Big Impact: Only appears at the start of the film, but is the one who sentenced Andy to 2 consecutive life sentences. Stern Old Judge: He lectures Andy and is vicious in his condemnation of the man he honestly believes to be a cold-blooded murderer.

characters.name: 1966 D.A.
characters.description: The district attorney at the time of Andy's escape. He brings the indictments against Norton and his conspirators. Be Careful What You Wish For: He tells Norton through his locked office door to "make it easy on yourself" when they attempt to arrest him. This leads Norton to turn his gun away from the door and on himself. Granted, this probably saved him from being shot by Norton upon entering the room. Cool Old Guy: An older prosecutor keen to see Norton and Hadley pay for their crimes. No Name Given: Like his predecessor, his name is never mentioned. Small Role, Big Impact: Appears only towards the end of the movie, but he is the one who places Hadley under arrest and causes Norton to shoot himself.

characters.name: Jake
characters.description: Brooks pet crow whom he's been nursing since he fell out of his nest by the plate shop as a chick and injured his wing. Adaptation Species Change: Is a pigeon in the novella, with the film changing him to a crow. What Happened to the Mouse?: The last we see of him is when Brooks sets him loose before he leaves after being paroled. Given he'd been hand-fed by Brooks from a young age, he wouldn't have the survival skills a crow would learn in the wild and likely didn't last long on the outside. A deleted scene has Red finding his body.

characters.name: Ned Grimes
characters.description: An old friend of Norton's who runs a construction business. What Happened to the Mouse?: It's unknown what becomes of him after Andy escapes and exposes Norton's dealings by mailing his ledger to the Portland Daily Bugle. The byline for the front page article about Shawshank's corruption mentions the D.A. has the ledger and that indictments are expected, so if Ned's name was mentioned, especially if he had further dealings with Norton after bribing him for the highway contract, he was most likely arrested.
